project_overview.project_name: Guinea Natural Resources, Mining and Environmental Management Project
project_overview.project_development_objective: Strengthen institutional capacities for integrated management of mineral and natural resources in Guinea and enhanced benefits from the mining and environment sectors
project_overview.total_financing: US$65.00 million (SDR 46.0 million equivalent: SDR 23.0 million Credit + SDR 23.0 million Grant)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Ministry of Mining and Geology (MMG), Ministry of Environment, Water and Forests (MEEF), local communities in mining and protected area regions (approximately 1 million people), women and youth in mining communities, SMEs/SMIs, civil society organizations, mining companies, and the general Guinean population

primary_locations: Upper Niger National Park (Mafou and Kouya districts)
Guinea Forest region (Pic de Fon, Mount Nimba, Diécké, Mount Béro)
Marine protected areas (Rio Pongo, Rio Kapatchez, Konkouré, Loos Islands)
geographic_scope.coverage_type: National with focus on specific mining regions and protected areas
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: Partial - Guinea is classified as a Fragile State. The country faces political tensions, weak governance, and human rights concerns. However, the project areas are not in active conflict zones but in regions affected by mining activities and environmental degradation.

key_components.component_name: Component A: Improve the institutional framework for mining and environment
key_components.description: Strengthen institutional capacities and intersectoral collaboration through: (A1) coordination of mining and environment strategy and regulation including harmonization of sectoral regulations and SESA monitoring; (A2) tools to enhance mining-environment coordination including land-use planning, sustainable financing for conservation, and water monitoring systems; (A3) strengthening Guinea's capacity on climate change commitments including GHG inventory development and NDC implementation
key_components.financing: US$5.00 million (US$3.00 million to MMG, US$2.00 million to MEEF)

key_components.component_name: Component B: Mining policies, institutions, governance and economic integration
key_components.description: Support mining sector governance through: (B1) mining policies, institutions and access to resources including cadaster modernization, geological services, capacity building of mining institutions, and advisory services; (B2) economic integration of mining sector including local content promotion through online platform (BSTP), Chamber of Mines support, and transparency/citizen engagement initiatives

key_components.component_name: Component C: Environmental and natural resources management
key_components.description: Improve MEEF capacity through: (C1) institutional, logistical support and ESS management including BGACE-OGPR office building, technology systems, and capacity building in environmental and social standards; (C2) protected area management in selected areas including knowledge/training, infrastructure/equipment, participatory management, and alternative income-generating activities for local communities
Finance operational costs of Project Implementation Unit (PIU) including staff recruitment and training, operating costs, monitoring and evaluation, audit, and communications

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Mining and Geology (MMG) and Ministry of Environment, Water and Forests (MEEF) with a Project Implementation Unit (PIU) based in MMG
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Steering Committee (bi-annual meetings, chaired by Secretary General of MMG with MEEF Secretary General as deputy); Technical Monitoring Committee (monthly meetings, chaired by Director General of BSD of MEEF); coordination with Niger Basin Authority (ABN) through Memorandum of Understanding
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank's Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers (NPF); two procurement plans for MMG and MEEF activities; use of STEP online procurement tracking system; prior review for contracts above thresholds; competitive procurement methods; estimated substantial procurement risk due to limited MEEF experience with World Bank procedures
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Standard World Bank practices with quarterly Progress Reports; M&E system involves local communities and civil society; use of Geo-Enabling Initiative for Monitoring and Supervision (GEMS) being considered; PDO-level indicators tracked by MMG, BGACE, and OGPR focal points; intermediate results indicators tracked through PIU and technical specialists

identified_risks.risk_description: Political tensions and weak governance represent substantial risk. Despite recent elections, political tensions and human rights allegations remain. Political risks may impact due processes in mining sector governance, particularly regarding allocation of licenses and their development.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Continued engagement with Government at various levels; coordination between ministries; regular technical and financial partner meetings; engagement with civil society organizations; monitoring of political situation with advice from World Bank Management

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial risk that sector strategies may not be fully implemented. While Government has prioritized local content and environmental protection, more efforts needed to enable wider citizen benefits from mining. Protection of environment was not historically a government priority.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Government awareness of shortcomings and prioritization of local content in PNDES; elevation of environment sector with Minister of State in charge; MEEF institutional reforms and increased annual budget; project support to SME/SMI capacities through Online Platform; complementary World Bank projects on local development and agribusiness

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial risk due to multiplicity of tasks and diverging experience/capacities at MMG and MEEF. Enhancing mineral benefits for more citizens presents new challenges. MEEF has less World Bank experience than MMG.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Nature of activities remains relatively upstream and capacity-building focused; MMG has prior World Bank support to build on; MEEF has received donor support from other partners; mobilization of required technical assistance; incorporation of lessons from successful World Bank projects (PACV, etc.)

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Moderate risk for MMG given MGSP experience; substantial risk for MEEF which has weak capacity and limited/irregular budgets. MEEF's first-of-a-kind World Bank operation. Limited capacity to cover country-wide needs.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: MMG leadership in project implementation; use of existing PIU familiar with MEEF from MGSP; careful assessment of new recruits; maintenance of key staff from well-performing MGSP PIU; capacity building program for MEEF on project management, technical, fiduciary, M&E and communication aspects; institutional assessment of BGACE and OGPR; mid-term review to assess capacity transfer possibilities

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial FM risk due to: multiplicity of actors resulting in large number of transactions; beneficiaries in geographically remote areas; transfers of funds to implementing agencies with limited capacity; MEEF's lack of familiarity with World Bank procedures; country's fragile PFM system with identified weaknesses in PEFA and PIMA assessments
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Use of ring-fenced financing mechanism with PIU; two designated accounts (DA-A for MMG, DA-B for MEEF); monthly justification of expenditures; adoption of FM procedures manual within one month of effectiveness; installation of accounting software within three months; recruitment of dedicated accountant for MEEF FM within one month; creation of internal audit function within six months; quarterly interim financial reports; annual audited financial statements; capacity building program for MEEF FM staff

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial procurement risk. Only MMG has experience with World Bank-financed projects; MEEF has little experience. Limited procurement skills and insufficient experience in World Bank procedures among staff and tender committees. Significant time delays in procurement process. Multiple government actors involved.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Maintain Procurement Specialist in PIU or recruit competitively; elaborate PIM with procurement procedures within one month of effectiveness; recruit Procurement Analyst within one month; provide procurement training to tender committee members within six months; use of STEP online tracking system; prior review of contracts above thresholds; detailed procurement plan covering first 18 months

identified_risks.risk_description: Overall E&S risk is substantial. Environment risk is moderate - project will build government capacity and provide funding for improved natural capital management. Social risk is substantial due to mining context of country. Potential impacts from protected area management implementation, office building construction, and limited government experience with ESF.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Use of same PIU as MGSP which had moderately satisfactory E&S rating; addition of social development specialist to PIU; evaluation of mining SESA within six months of effectiveness; implementation of World Bank's ESF and other international standards; preparation of ESIA for office building; development of ESMF, SEP, LMP, and Process Framework; establishment of grievance redress mechanisms; participatory approach with local communities; gender-sensitive project design

identified_risks.risk_description: Moderate risk linked to country's dependence on global economy, institutional and financial constraints, COVID-19 uncertainties, policy slippages, and political environment. Decline in global commodity prices or growth slowdown in China/advanced economies would significantly impact Guinea's economy. Fiscal slippages could lead to inflationary financing.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project support to diversification of mined commodities; improved capacities in Ministry of Budget for revenue mobilization; World Bank budget support operations; Government's track record of successful stabilization and structural reforms; Extended Credit Facility with IMF (December 2017-December 2020); Government response to health and economic crisis supported by donor community

identified_risks.risk_description: Moderate risk based on potential impact on PDO. Biggest exposure around communities where income-generating activities are planned. Private sector may be wary of stronger MEEF environmental standards.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Use of existing participatory decision-making structures from PACV; involvement of local communities in public consultations and management activities; regular coordination meetings with technical and financial partners; CSO involvement and support; outreach to private sector to explain benefits of stronger environmental standards

triggered_safeguard_policies: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts (ESS1)
Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management (ESS3)
Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement (ESS5)
Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources (ESS6)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Project-level GRM established building on existing mechanisms from PACV project; local GRM mechanisms in each commune; participatory management committees; World Bank's corporate Grievance Redress Service (GRS) available; World Bank Inspection Panel available for complaints
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Significant gender focus: Project aims to close gender gaps in women's voice/agency and access to income. Targets 70% of alternative income-generating activities (AIGAs) to benefit women. Aims to increase women's participation as decision-makers in protected area governance committees. Includes indicator on 'Share of women represented in local decision-making committees' with target of 20% by project end. Supports women's organizations and networks in mining sector. Addresses gender gaps in mining sector including lack of sector-specific gender policy, declining girls in high school, insufficient capacities for economic initiatives, and inadequate participation in CSR activities. Mining sector gender strategy to be drafted.
vulnerable_groups: Poor and vulnerable populations dependent on natural resources for livelihoods
Local communities in and around protected areas (approximately 1 million people)
Women in mining and protected area communities
Indigenous and community heritage area residents

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project is designed with conflict sensitivity in mind. Guinea is classified as a Fragile State with political tensions and weak governance. The project addresses upstream institutional capacity building rather than direct service delivery, which reduces conflict exposure. Project includes transparency and citizen engagement mechanisms to reduce grievances. Coordination with civil society and accountability agencies supports conflict prevention. However, mining sector governance remains fragile and subject to political economy pressures from powerful vested interests.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Guinea is a low-income fragile state with: (1) Political instability - recent elections but ongoing political tensions and human rights allegations; (2) Weak governance - PEFA and PIMA assessments identified critical PFM weaknesses; CPIA rating indicates governance concerns; (3) Institutional weakness - limited capacity of government agencies, particularly MEEF; (4) Economic vulnerability - heavy dependence on mining (35% of GDP), commodity price volatility, COVID-19 impacts; (5) Social vulnerability - 44% poverty rate, 18% food insecurity, 26% chronic malnutrition, high gender inequality; (6) Environmental degradation - negative adjusted net savings (-48% of GNI), deforestation rate increased from 0.2% to 2.8%, ecological footprint declining; (7) Post-crisis context - recovery from Ebola (2014-15), now facing COVID-19 and new Ebola outbreak
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Project activities are in regions with mining operations and protected areas, not in active conflict zones. However, security risks include: (1) Weak state capacity to enforce regulations in remote areas; (2) Artisanal mining activities that may involve informal armed groups; (3) Potential for resource-based conflicts over land use between mining and conservation; (4) General insecurity in remote protected area regions affecting ranger safety; (5) COVID-19 and Ebola outbreak impacts on implementation. Mitigation includes: use of experienced PIU, coordination with local authorities, community engagement, and adaptive management approaches.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: No major displacement expected from project activities. However, project may affect access to natural resources in protected areas: (1) Protected area management plans may restrict access to forest products, grazing, and agricultural land in buffer zones; (2) Process Framework prepared to address restrictions on land use and resource access; (3) Alternative income-generating activities (AIGAs) designed to compensate communities for forgone income from restricted activities; (4) Participatory approach with local communities in planning and implementation; (5) Existing community structures (ANAFIC committees) used for participatory management; (6) Project targets 500 beneficiaries with increased income from AIGAs by project end, with 350 being women. No involuntary resettlement anticipated, but land use restrictions require careful management and community consultation.
